Eliot Contents “Written in Water” by Robert Ford “The Haunted Houses She Calls Her Own” by Gwendolyn Kiste “Mirror, Mirror” by Joshua Palmatier “Phoenix” by Mark Allan Gunnells “The Animal of Our Bodies” by Kristi DeMeester “O Adelín” by Michael Wehunt “Sharp Echoes” by Kelli Owen “Womb With a View” by Chad Lutzke “Midnight in the Southland” by Todd Keisling “Reverend Crow” by Joanna Koch “Rotten to the Core” by Richard Thomas “Back to One” by Jessica McHugh “Cabinet People” by Norman Prentiss “The Black Door” by Anthony J. Rapino Afterword by Kevin Lucia Preview Story: “A Mother is a Kind of God” by Eric LaRocca When I first read Robert Ford's short story “Georgie” (which I bought for Shroud Magazine's Halloween Issue so many years ago), I felt like my heart had been dug out of my chest with a dull ice cream scoop. It was not only a finely written Halloween ghost story, but it throbbed with an aching emotion I hadn't seen much of in horror fiction at that point. Since then, Robert's work has never failed to deliver an emotional gut-punch right to the center of my being. He has a knack for finding those secret pressure points we all have, and delivering just the right amount of pressure, driving us to the edge, which I'm sure he'll do with “Written in Water.” His most recent novel is Burner, one of the finest extreme-horror novels I've ever read. WRITTEN IN WATER by Robert Ford I ’m sitting, crisscross-applesauce, in the deepest part of the deepest end of the Twin Oaks Community Center swimming pool, but down here, I may as well be sitting on Mars. My eyes are closed, have been closed, for about five minutes. I’ve never been able to open my eyes underwater. It makes me uncomfortable. It’s not the pressure against them, it’s that everything is blurry and out of focus. It’s like I’m viewing the world as I’m dying, as an almost-ghost, caught between the living and the dead. It makes me uncomfortable, and so I keep my eyes closed. I sit very still in the darkness, letting my limp arms drift with any current the water might carry. I wait for my twin-sister Della to reach from the shadows and touch my shoulder. We do this thing that bonds us together so tightly. Della keeps me alive. Literally. I take a deep breath and swim to the previously mentioned deepest of deeps, sit down like a Yogi Mystic, and slowly let the air seep out from between my lips. Sometimes the bubbles catch my nostrils on the way up and tickle me, but I keep myself from laughing. I can feel Della doing her best mermaid impersonation. I can feel the rush of water as she kicks, and I imagine she’s a real mermaid. It makes me smile, even though no one will see my expression. When my lungs are almost empty, I stop feeling Della swimming around me, but a moment later, right as I start to burn inside, I feel her hand softly land on my left shoulder, and then her lips are on mine, gently pushing the air from her lungs into me. After a moment, she swims back to the surface, takes another breath, and comes down to do it again. Over and over. I could be down here for an hour, kept alive from this connection with my sister. It’s an unbelievably powerful feeling. I miss Della. I miss her so much. When she died, I saw her lying in that hospital bed and I wanted to put my lips on hers and breathe air into her lungs to bring her back to life, but my father stopped me. He said it wasn’t the right thing to do, that it wouldn’t help, but I still wonder. # I’m in Mrs. Eckenrode’s Advanced Biology class. She uses an erasable marker on the whiteboard at the front of the room, and draws the parts of a mushroom. It looks like a UFO to me, or a distorted version of the worksheets we were given in Sex-Ed. That thought makes me stifle a giggle and, from across the room, I can feel Della turn toward me. We’re never allowed to sit together in any of our classes. Her expression is amused, and I wonder, as I often do, if we occasionally have the same thoughts at the same time. She raises an eyebrow questioningly and I glance at the front of the room to make sure Mrs. Eckenrode’s back is still to us. It is, and I point to the drawing of the UFO/penis on the whiteboard. I look back at Della, make a fist with my right hand, and get ready to push my tongue into my left cheek to the internationally recognized mimic
